cwes.name: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm
cwes.desc: The product uses a broken or risky cryptographic algorithm or protocol.

Embedded Database._note: Lightweight database embedded in the vehicle system. Examples: SQLite (logs, configuration), Redis (cache), proprietary flash filesystems. SQL injection or direct file access can leak sensitive data (driving history, GPS logs).

cwes.name: Compiler Removal of Code to Clear Buffers
cwes.desc: Sensitive memory is cleared according to the source code, but compiler optimizations leave the memory untouched when it is not read from again, aka dead store removal.

cwes.name: Improper Restriction of Operations within the Bounds of a Memory Buffer
cwes.desc: The product performs operations on a memory buffer, but it reads from or writes to a memory location outside the buffer's intended boundary. This may result in read or write operations on unexpected m
cwes.description: Buffer operations without bounds checking allow memory corruption.

cwes.name: Buffer Copy without Checking Size of Input ('Classic Buffer Overflow')
cwes.desc: The product copies an input buffer to an output buffer without verifying that the size of the input buffer is less than the size of the output buffer.

cwes.name: Stack-based Buffer Overflow
cwes.desc: A stack-based buffer overflow condition is a condition where the buffer being overwritten is allocated on the stack (i.e., is a local variable or, rarely, a parameter to a function).

cwes.name: Heap-based Buffer Overflow
cwes.desc: A heap overflow condition is a buffer overflow, where the buffer that can be overwritten is allocated in the heap portion of memory, generally meaning that the buffer was allocated using a routine suc

cwes.name: Write-what-where Condition
cwes.desc: Any condition where the attacker has the ability to write an arbitrary value to an arbitrary location, often as the result of a buffer overflow.

cwes.name: Buffer Underwrite ('Buffer Underflow')
cwes.desc: The product writes to a buffer using an index or pointer that references a memory location prior to the beginning of the buffer.

cwes.name: Out-of-bounds Read
cwes.desc: The product reads data past the end, or before the beginning, of the intended buffer.
cwes.description: Read beyond allocated buffer boundary; information disclosure or crash.

cwes.name: Buffer Over-read
cwes.desc: The product reads from a buffer using buffer access mechanisms such as indexes or pointers that reference memory locations after the targeted buffer.

cwes.name: Buffer Under-read
cwes.desc: The product reads from a buffer using buffer access mechanisms such as indexes or pointers that reference memory locations prior to the targeted buffer.

cwes.name: Wrap-around Error
cwes.desc: Wrap around errors occur whenever a value is incremented past the maximum value for its type and therefore wraps around to a very small, negative, or undefined value.

cwes.name: Improper Validation of Array Index
cwes.desc: The product uses untrusted input when calculating or using an array index, but the product does not validate or incorrectly validates the index to ensure the index references a valid position within t

cwes.name: Improper Handling of Length Parameter Inconsistency
cwes.desc: The product parses a formatted message or structure, but it does not handle or incorrectly handles a length field that is inconsistent with the actual length of the associated data.

cwes.name: Incorrect Calculation of Buffer Size
cwes.desc: The product does not correctly calculate the size to be used when allocating a buffer, which could lead to a buffer overflow.

cwes.name: Use of Externally-Controlled Format String
cwes.desc: The product uses a function that accepts a format string as an argument, but the format string originates from an external source.

cwes.name: Incorrect Calculation of Multi-Byte String Length
cwes.desc: The product does not correctly calculate the length of strings that can contain wide or multi-byte characters.

cwes.name: Improper Neutralization of Null Byte or NUL Character
cwes.desc: The product receives input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes NUL characters or null bytes when they are sent to a downstream component.

cwes.name: Improper Null Termination
cwes.desc: The product does not terminate or incorrectly terminates a string or array with a null character or equivalent terminator.

cwes.name: Reliance on Data/Memory Layout
cwes.desc: The product makes invalid assumptions about how protocol data or memory is organized at a lower level, resulting in unintended program behavior.

cwes.name: Integer Overflow or Wraparound
cwes.desc: The product performs a calculation that can produce an integer overflow or wraparound when the logic assumes that the resulting value will always be larger than the original value. This occurs when an
cwes.description: Integer arithmetic overflow wraps around, causing unexpected behavior.

cwes.name: Integer Underflow (Wrap or Wraparound)
cwes.desc: The product subtracts one value from another, such that the result is less than the minimum allowable integer value, which produces a value that is not equal to the correct result.

cwes.name: Integer Coercion Error
cwes.desc: Integer coercion refers to a set of flaws pertaining to the type casting, extension, or truncation of primitive data types.

cwes.name: Off-by-one Error
cwes.desc: A product calculates or uses an incorrect maximum or minimum value that is 1 more, or 1 less, than the correct value.

cwes.name: Unexpected Sign Extension
cwes.desc: The product performs an operation on a number that causes it to be sign extended when it is transformed into a larger data type. When the original number is negative, this can produce unexpected value

cwes.name: Signed to Unsigned Conversion Error
cwes.desc: The product uses a signed primitive and performs a cast to an unsigned primitive, which can produce an unexpected value if the value of the signed primitive can not be represented using an unsigned pr

cwes.name: Unsigned to Signed Conversion Error
cwes.desc: The product uses an unsigned primitive and performs a cast to a signed primitive, which can produce an unexpected value if the value of the unsigned primitive can not be represented using a signed pri

cwes.name: Numeric Truncation Error
cwes.desc: Truncation errors occur when a primitive is cast to a primitive of a smaller size and data is lost in the conversion.

cwes.name: Use of Inherently Dangerous Function
cwes.desc: The product calls a function that can never be guaranteed to work safely.

cwes.name: Creation of chroot Jail Without Changing Working Directory
cwes.desc: The product uses the chroot() system call to create a jail, but does not change the working directory afterward. This does not prevent access to files outside of the jail.

cwes.name: Improper Clearing of Heap Memory Before Release ('Heap Inspection')
cwes.desc: Using realloc() to resize buffers that store sensitive information can leave the sensitive information exposed to attack, because it is not removed from memory.

cwes.name: Uncaught Exception
cwes.desc: An exception is thrown from a function, but it is not caught.

cwes.name: Concurrent Execution using Shared Resource with Improper Synchronization ('Race Condition')
cwes.desc: The product contains a concurrent code sequence that requires temporary, exclusive access to a shared resource, but a timing window exists in which the shared resource can be modified by another code
cwes.description: Race condition in concurrent code leads to unexpected state transitions.

cwes.name: Signal Handler Race Condition
cwes.desc: The product uses a signal handler that introduces a race condition.

cwes.name: Race Condition within a Thread
cwes.desc: If two threads of execution use a resource simultaneously, there exists the possibility that resources may be used while invalid, in turn making the state of execution undefined.

cwes.name: Passing Mutable Objects to an Untrusted Method
cwes.desc: The product sends non-cloned mutable data as an argument to a method or function.

cwes.name: Returning a Mutable Object to an Untrusted Caller
cwes.desc: Sending non-cloned mutable data as a return value may result in that data being altered or deleted by the calling function.

cwes.name: Declaration of Catch for Generic Exception
cwes.desc: Catching overly broad exceptions promotes complex error handling code that is more likely to contain security vulnerabilities.

cwes.name: Declaration of Throws for Generic Exception
cwes.desc: The product throws or raises an overly broad exceptions that can hide important details and produce inappropriate responses to certain conditions.

cwes.name: Missing Release of Memory after Effective Lifetime
cwes.desc: The product does not sufficiently track and release allocated memory after it has been used, making the memory unavailable for reallocation and reuse.

cwes.name: Exposure of File Descriptor to Unintended Control Sphere ('File Descriptor Leak')
cwes.desc: A process does not close sensitive file descriptors before invoking a child process, which allows the child to perform unauthorized I/O operations using those descriptors.

cwes.name: Double Free
cwes.desc: The product calls free() twice on the same memory address.

cwes.name: Use After Free
cwes.desc: The product reuses or references memory after it has been freed. At some point afterward, the memory may be allocated again and saved in another pointer, while the original pointer references a locati
cwes.description: Memory use after it has been freed leads to crash or code execution.

cwes.name: Use of Uninitialized Variable
cwes.desc: The code uses a variable that has not been initialized, leading to unpredictable or unintended results.

cwes.name: Improper Cleanup on Thrown Exception
cwes.desc: The product does not clean up its state or incorrectly cleans up its state when an exception is thrown, leading to unexpected state or control flow.

cwes.name: Duplicate Key in Associative List (Alist)
cwes.desc: Duplicate keys in associative lists can lead to non-unique keys being mistaken for an error.

cwes.name: Deletion of Data Structure Sentinel
cwes.desc: The accidental deletion of a data-structure sentinel can cause serious programming logic problems.

cwes.name: Addition of Data Structure Sentinel
cwes.desc: The accidental addition of a data-structure sentinel can cause serious programming logic problems.

cwes.name: Return of Pointer Value Outside of Expected Range
cwes.desc: A function can return a pointer to memory that is outside of the buffer that the pointer is expected to reference.

cwes.name: Use of sizeof() on a Pointer Type
cwes.desc: The code calls sizeof() on a pointer type, which can be an incorrect calculation if the programmer intended to determine the size of the data that is being pointed to.

cwes.name: Incorrect Pointer Scaling
cwes.desc: In C and C++, one may often accidentally refer to the wrong memory due to the semantics of when math operations are implicitly scaled.

cwes.name: Use of Pointer Subtraction to Determine Size
cwes.desc: The product subtracts one pointer from another in order to determine size, but this calculation can be incorrect if the pointers do not exist in the same memory chunk.

cwes.name: Use of Function with Inconsistent Implementations
cwes.desc: The code uses a function that has inconsistent implementations across operating systems and versions.

cwes.name: NULL Pointer Dereference
cwes.desc: The product dereferences a pointer that it expects to be valid but is NULL.
cwes.description: NULL pointer dereference causes application crash.

cwes.name: Missing Default Case in Multiple Condition Expression
cwes.desc: The code does not have a default case in an expression with multiple conditions, such as a switch statement.

cwes.name: Signal Handler Use of a Non-reentrant Function
cwes.desc: The product defines a signal handler that calls a non-reentrant function.

cwes.name: Use of Incorrect Operator
cwes.desc: The product accidentally uses the wrong operator, which changes the logic in security-relevant ways.

cwes.name: Assigning instead of Comparing
cwes.desc: The code uses an operator for assignment when the intention was to perform a comparison.

cwes.name: Comparing instead of Assigning
cwes.desc: The code uses an operator for comparison when the intention was to perform an assignment.

cwes.name: Incorrect Block Delimitation
cwes.desc: The code does not explicitly delimit a block that is intended to contain 2 or more statements, creating a logic error.

cwes.name: Omitted Break Statement in Switch
cwes.desc: The product omits a break statement within a switch or similar construct, causing code associated with multiple conditions to execute. This can cause problems when the programmer only intended to exec

cwes.name: Critical Public Variable Without Final Modifier
cwes.desc: The product has a critical public variable that is not final, which allows the variable to be modified to contain unexpected values.

cwes.name: Private Data Structure Returned From A Public Method
cwes.desc: The product has a method that is declared public, but returns a reference to a private data structure, which could then be modified in unexpected ways.

cwes.name: Public Data Assigned to Private Array-Typed Field
cwes.desc: Assigning public data to a private array is equivalent to giving public access to the array.

cwes.name: Cloneable Class Containing Sensitive Information
cwes.desc: The code contains a class with sensitive data, but the class is cloneable. The data can then be accessed by cloning the class.

cwes.name: Public Static Field Not Marked Final
cwes.desc: An object contains a public static field that is not marked final, which might allow it to be modified in unexpected ways.

cwes.name: Use of Singleton Pattern Without Synchronization in a Multithreaded Context
cwes.desc: The product uses the singleton pattern when creating a resource within a multithreaded environment.

cwes.name: Use of getlogin() in Multithreaded Application
cwes.desc: The product uses the getlogin() function in a multithreaded context, potentially causing it to return incorrect values.

cwes.name: Use of umask() with chmod-style Argument
cwes.desc: The product calls umask() with an incorrect argument that is specified as if it is an argument to chmod().

cwes.name: Return of Stack Variable Address
cwes.desc: A function returns the address of a stack variable, which will cause unintended program behavior, typically in the form of a crash.

cwes.name: Assignment of a Fixed Address to a Pointer
cwes.desc: The product sets a pointer to a specific address other than NULL or 0.

cwes.name: Attempt to Access Child of a Non-structure Pointer
cwes.desc: Casting a non-structure type to a structure type and accessing a field can lead to memory access errors or data corruption.

cwes.name: Free of Memory not on the Heap
cwes.desc: The product calls free() on a pointer to memory that was not allocated using associated heap allocation functions such as malloc(), calloc(), or realloc().

cwes.name: Reachable Assertion
cwes.desc: The product contains an assert() or similar statement that can be triggered by an attacker, which leads to an application exit or other behavior that is more severe than necessary.

cwes.name: Use of a Non-reentrant Function in a Concurrent Context
cwes.desc: The product calls a non-reentrant function in a concurrent context in which a competing code sequence (e.g. thread or signal handler) may have an opportunity to call the same function or otherwise inf

cwes.name: Use of Potentially Dangerous Function
cwes.desc: The product invokes a potentially dangerous function that could introduce a vulnerability if it is used incorrectly, but the function can also be used safely.

cwes.name: Integer Overflow to Buffer Overflow
cwes.desc: The product performs a calculation to determine how much memory to allocate, but an integer overflow can occur that causes less memory to be allocated than expected, leading to a buffer overflow.

cwes.name: Incorrect Conversion between Numeric Types
cwes.desc: When converting from one data type to another, such as long to integer, data can be omitted or translated in a way that produces unexpected values. If the resulting values are used in a sensitive cont

cwes.name: Function Call With Incorrect Number of Arguments
cwes.desc: The product calls a function, procedure, or routine, but the caller specifies too many arguments, or too few arguments, which may lead to undefined behavior and resultant weaknesses.

cwes.name: Function Call With Incorrect Variable or Reference as Argument
cwes.desc: The product calls a function, procedure, or routine, but the caller specifies the wrong variable or reference as one of the arguments, which may lead to undefined behavior and resultant weaknesses.

cwes.name: Permission Race Condition During Resource Copy
cwes.desc: The product, while copying or cloning a resource, does not set the resource's permissions or access control until the copy is complete, leaving the resource exposed to other spheres while the copy is

cwes.name: Unchecked Return Value to NULL Pointer Dereference
cwes.desc: The product does not check for an error after calling a function that can return with a NULL pointer if the function fails, which leads to a resultant NULL pointer dereference.

cwes.name: Incorrect Type Conversion or Cast
cwes.desc: The product does not correctly convert an object, resource, or structure from one type to a different type.

cwes.name: Compiler Optimization Removal or Modification of Security-critical Code
cwes.desc: The developer builds a security-critical protection mechanism into the software, but the compiler optimizes the program such that the mechanism is removed or modified.

cwes.name: Free of Pointer not at Start of Buffer
cwes.desc: The product calls free() on a pointer to a memory resource that was allocated on the heap, but the pointer is not at the start of the buffer.

cwes.name: Mismatched Memory Management Routines
cwes.desc: The product attempts to return a memory resource to the system, but it calls a release function that is not compatible with the function that was originally used to allocate that resource.

cwes.name: Release of Invalid Pointer or Reference
cwes.desc: The product attempts to return a memory resource to the system, but it calls the wrong release function or calls the appropriate release function incorrectly.

cwes.name: Critical Data Element Declared Public
cwes.desc: The product declares a critical variable, field, or member to be public when intended security policy requires it to be private.

cwes.name: Access to Critical Private Variable via Public Method
cwes.desc: The product defines a public method that reads or modifies a private variable.

cwes.name: Incorrect Short Circuit Evaluation
cwes.desc: The product contains a conditional statement with multiple logical expressions in which one of the non-leading expressions may produce side effects. This may lead to an unexpected state in the program

cwes.name: Improper Address Validation in IOCTL with METHOD_NEITHER I/O Control Code
cwes.desc: The product defines an IOCTL that uses METHOD_NEITHER for I/O, but it does not validate or incorrectly validates the addresses that are provided.

cwes.name: Exposed IOCTL with Insufficient Access Control
cwes.desc: The product implements an IOCTL with functionality that should be restricted, but it does not properly enforce access control for the IOCTL.

cwes.name: Operator Precedence Logic Error
cwes.desc: The product uses an expression in which operator precedence causes incorrect logic to be used.

cwes.name: Use of Path Manipulation Function without Maximum-sized Buffer
cwes.desc: The product invokes a function for normalizing paths or file names, but it provides an output buffer that is smaller than the maximum possible size, such as PATH_MAX.

cwes.name: Access of Memory Location Before Start of Buffer
cwes.desc: The product reads or writes to a buffer using an index or pointer that references a memory location prior to the beginning of the buffer.

cwes.name: Out-of-bounds Write
cwes.desc: The product writes data past the end, or before the beginning, of the intended buffer.
cwes.description: Write operation beyond allocated buffer; memory corruption / code execution.

cwes.name: Access of Memory Location After End of Buffer
cwes.desc: The product reads or writes to a buffer using an index or pointer that references a memory location after the end of the buffer.

cwes.name: Memory Allocation with Excessive Size Value
cwes.desc: The product allocates memory based on an untrusted, large size value, but it does not ensure that the size is within expected limits, allowing arbitrary amounts of memory to be allocated.

cwes.name: Buffer Access with Incorrect Length Value
cwes.desc: The product uses a sequential operation to read or write a buffer, but it uses an incorrect length value that causes it to access memory that is outside of the bounds of the buffer.

cwes.name: Buffer Access Using Size of Source Buffer
cwes.desc: The product uses the size of a source buffer when reading from or writing to a destination buffer, which may cause it to access memory that is outside of the bounds of the buffer.

cwes.name: Untrusted Pointer Dereference
cwes.desc: The product obtains a value from an untrusted source, converts this value to a pointer, and dereferences the resulting pointer.

cwes.name: Use of Out-of-range Pointer Offset
cwes.desc: The product performs pointer arithmetic on a valid pointer, but it uses an offset that can point outside of the intended range of valid memory locations for the resulting pointer.

cwes.name: Access of Uninitialized Pointer
cwes.desc: The product accesses or uses a pointer that has not been initialized.

cwes.name: Expired Pointer Dereference
cwes.desc: The product dereferences a pointer that contains a location for memory that was previously valid, but is no longer valid.

cwes.name: Signal Handler with Functionality that is not Asynchronous-Safe
cwes.desc: The product defines a signal handler that contains code sequences that are not asynchronous-safe, i.e., the functionality is not reentrant, or it can be interrupted.

cwes.name: Numeric Range Comparison Without Minimum Check
cwes.desc: The product checks a value to ensure that it is less than or equal to a maximum, but it does not also verify that the value is greater than or equal to the minimum.

cwes.name: Access of Resource Using Incompatible Type ('Type Confusion')
cwes.desc: The product allocates or initializes a resource such as a pointer, object, or variable using one type, but it later accesses that resource using a type that is incompatible with the original type.

cwes.name: Use of Expired File Descriptor
cwes.desc: The product uses or accesses a file descriptor after it has been closed.

cwes.name: Improper Update of Reference Count
cwes.desc: The product uses a reference count to manage a resource, but it does not update or incorrectly updates the reference count.

cwes.name: Improperly Controlled Sequential Memory Allocation
cwes.desc: The product manages a group of objects or resources and performs a separate memory allocation for each object, but it does not properly limit the total amount of memory that is consumed by all of the

cwes.name: Incorrect Bitwise Shift of Integer
cwes.desc: An integer value is specified to be shifted by a negative amount or an amount greater than or equal to the number of bits contained in the value causing an unexpected or indeterminate result.

cwes.name: Multiple Releases of Same Resource or Handle
cwes.desc: The product attempts to close or release a resource or handle more than once, without any successful open between the close operations.

cwes.name: Missing Security-Relevant Feedback for Unexecuted Operations in Hardware Interface
cwes.desc: The product has a hardware interface that silently discards operations in situations for which feedback would be security-relevant, such as the timely detection of failures or attacks.

PID-3121: Object oriented programming la.name: Device includes support for object oriented programming languages(e.g., Java, Python, PHP, C++)

cwes.name: Deserialization of Untrusted Data (Base)
cwes.desc: The product deserializes untrusted data without sufficiently ensuring that the resulting data will be valid.

PID-3122: Manual memory management langu.name: Device includes support for manual memory management programming languages (e.g. C, C++)

cwes.name: J2EE Misconfiguration: Data Transmission Without Encryption
cwes.desc: Information sent over a network can be compromised while in transit. An attacker may be able to read or modify the contents if the data are sent in plaintext or are weakly encrypted.

cwes.name: J2EE Misconfiguration: Insufficient Session-ID Length
cwes.desc: The J2EE application is configured to use an insufficient session ID length.

cwes.name: J2EE Misconfiguration: Missing Custom Error Page
cwes.desc: The default error page of a web application should not display sensitive information about the product.

cwes.name: J2EE Misconfiguration: Entity Bean Declared Remote
cwes.desc: When an application exposes a remote interface for an entity bean, it might also expose methods that get or set the bean's data. These methods could be leveraged to read sensitive information, or to c

cwes.name: J2EE Misconfiguration: Weak Access Permissions for EJB Methods
cwes.desc: If elevated access rights are assigned to EJB methods, then an attacker can take advantage of the permissions to exploit the product.

cwes.name: Improper Neutralization of Directives in Dynamically Evaluated Code ('Eval Injection')
cwes.desc: The product receives input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes code syntax before using the input in a dynamic evaluation call (e.g. eval).

cwes.name: Struts: Duplicate Validation Forms
cwes.desc: The product uses multiple validation forms with the same name, which might cause the Struts Validator to validate a form that the programmer does not expect.

cwes.name: Struts: Incomplete validate() Method Definition
cwes.desc: The product has a validator form that either does not define a validate() method, or defines a validate() method but does not call super.validate().

cwes.name: Struts: Form Bean Does Not Extend Validation Class
cwes.desc: If a form bean does not extend an ActionForm subclass of the Validator framework, it can expose the application to other weaknesses related to insufficient input validation.

cwes.name: Struts: Form Field Without Validator
cwes.desc: The product has a form field that is not validated by a corresponding validation form, which can introduce other weaknesses related to insufficient input validation.

cwes.name: Struts: Plug-in Framework not in Use
cwes.desc: When an application does not use an input validation framework such as the Struts Validator, there is a greater risk of introducing weaknesses related to insufficient input validation.

cwes.name: Struts: Unused Validation Form
cwes.desc: An unused validation form indicates that validation logic is not up-to-date.

cwes.name: Struts: Unvalidated Action Form
cwes.desc: Every Action Form must have a corresponding validation form.

cwes.name: Struts: Validator Turned Off
cwes.desc: Automatic filtering via a Struts bean has been turned off, which disables the Struts Validator and custom validation logic. This exposes the application to other weaknesses related to insufficient inp

cwes.name: Struts: Validator Without Form Field
cwes.desc: Validation fields that do not appear in forms they are associated with indicate that the validation logic is out of date.

cwes.name: Direct Use of Unsafe JNI
cwes.desc: When a Java application uses the Java Native Interface (JNI) to call code written in another programming language, it can expose the application to weaknesses in that code, even if those weaknesses ca

cwes.name: Integer Underflow (Wrap or Wraparound)
cwes.desc: The product subtracts one value from another, such that the result is less than the minimum allowable integer value, which produces a value that is not equal to the correct result.

cwes.name: Integer Coercion Error
cwes.desc: Integer coercion refers to a set of flaws pertaining to the type casting, extension, or truncation of primitive data types.

cwes.name: Numeric Truncation Error
cwes.desc: Truncation errors occur when a primitive is cast to a primitive of a smaller size and data is lost in the conversion.

cwes.name: Generation of Error Message Containing Sensitive Information
cwes.desc: The product generates an error message that includes sensitive information about its environment, users, or associated data.

cwes.name: J2EE Bad Practices: Direct Management of Connections
cwes.desc: The J2EE application directly manages connections, instead of using the container's connection management facilities.

cwes.name: J2EE Bad Practices: Direct Use of Sockets
cwes.desc: The J2EE application directly uses sockets instead of using framework method calls.

cwes.name: Uncaught Exception
cwes.desc: An exception is thrown from a function, but it is not caught.

cwes.name: Concurrent Execution using Shared Resource with Improper Synchronization ('Race Condition')
cwes.desc: The product contains a concurrent code sequence that requires temporary, exclusive access to a shared resource, but a timing window exists in which the shared resource can be modified by another code
cwes.description: Race condition in concurrent code leads to unexpected state transitions.

cwes.name: Race Condition within a Thread
cwes.desc: If two threads of execution use a resource simultaneously, there exists the possibility that resources may be used while invalid, in turn making the state of execution undefined.

cwes.name: Passing Mutable Objects to an Untrusted Method
cwes.desc: The product sends non-cloned mutable data as an argument to a method or function.

cwes.name: Returning a Mutable Object to an Untrusted Caller
cwes.desc: Sending non-cloned mutable data as a return value may result in that data being altered or deleted by the calling function.

cwes.name: J2EE Bad Practices: Use of System.exit()
cwes.desc: A J2EE application uses System.exit(), which also shuts down its container.

cwes.name: J2EE Bad Practices: Direct Use of Threads
cwes.desc: Thread management in a Web application is forbidden in some circumstances and is always highly error prone.

cwes.name: Use of NullPointerException Catch to Detect NULL Pointer Dereference
cwes.desc: Catching NullPointerException should not be used as an alternative to programmatic checks to prevent dereferencing a null pointer.

cwes.name: Declaration of Catch for Generic Exception
cwes.desc: Catching overly broad exceptions promotes complex error handling code that is more likely to contain security vulnerabilities.

cwes.name: Declaration of Throws for Generic Exception
cwes.desc: The product throws or raises an overly broad exceptions that can hide important details and produce inappropriate responses to certain conditions.

cwes.name: Improper Cleanup on Thrown Exception
cwes.desc: The product does not clean up its state or incorrectly cleans up its state when an exception is thrown, leading to unexpected state or control flow.

cwes.name: Duplicate Key in Associative List (Alist)
cwes.desc: Duplicate keys in associative lists can lead to non-unique keys being mistaken for an error.

cwes.name: Use of Externally-Controlled Input to Select Classes or Code ('Unsafe Reflection')
cwes.desc: The product uses external input with reflection to select which classes or code to use, but it does not sufficiently prevent the input from selecting improper classes or code.

cwes.name: NULL Pointer Dereference
cwes.desc: The product dereferences a pointer that it expects to be valid but is NULL.
cwes.description: NULL pointer dereference causes application crash.

cwes.name: Missing Default Case in Multiple Condition Expression
cwes.desc: The code does not have a default case in an expression with multiple conditions, such as a switch statement.

cwes.name: Assigning instead of Comparing
cwes.desc: The code uses an operator for assignment when the intention was to perform a comparison.

cwes.name: Omitted Break Statement in Switch
cwes.desc: The product omits a break statement within a switch or similar construct, causing code associated with multiple conditions to execute. This can cause problems when the programmer only intended to exec

cwes.name: Comparison of Classes by Name
cwes.desc: The product compares classes by name, which can cause it to use the wrong class when multiple classes can have the same name.

cwes.name: Reliance on Package-level Scope
cwes.desc: Java packages are not inherently closed; therefore, relying on them for code security is not a good practice.

cwes.name: Public cloneable() Method Without Final ('Object Hijack')
cwes.desc: A class has a cloneable() method that is not declared final, which allows an object to be created without calling the constructor. This can cause the object to be in an unexpected state.

cwes.name: Use of Inner Class Containing Sensitive Data
cwes.desc: Inner classes are translated into classes that are accessible at package scope and may expose code that the programmer intended to keep private to attackers.

cwes.name: Critical Public Variable Without Final Modifier
cwes.desc: The product has a critical public variable that is not final, which allows the variable to be modified to contain unexpected values.

cwes.name: Private Data Structure Returned From A Public Method
cwes.desc: The product has a method that is declared public, but returns a reference to a private data structure, which could then be modified in unexpected ways.

cwes.name: Public Data Assigned to Private Array-Typed Field
cwes.desc: Assigning public data to a private array is equivalent to giving public access to the array.

cwes.name: Cloneable Class Containing Sensitive Information
cwes.desc: The code contains a class with sensitive data, but the class is cloneable. The data can then be accessed by cloning the class.

cwes.name: Serializable Class Containing Sensitive Data
cwes.desc: The code contains a class with sensitive data, but the class does not explicitly deny serialization. The data can be accessed by serializing the class through another class.

cwes.name: Public Static Field Not Marked Final
cwes.desc: An object contains a public static field that is not marked final, which might allow it to be modified in unexpected ways.

cwes.name: Deserialization of Untrusted Data
cwes.desc: The product deserializes untrusted data without sufficiently ensuring that the resulting data will be valid.

cwes.name: Servlet Runtime Error Message Containing Sensitive Information
cwes.desc: A servlet error message indicates that there exists an unhandled exception in the web application code and may provide useful information to an attacker.

cwes.name: Java Runtime Error Message Containing Sensitive Information
cwes.desc: In many cases, an attacker can leverage the conditions that cause unhandled exception errors in order to gain unauthorized access to the system.

cwes.name: Use of Singleton Pattern Without Synchronization in a Multithreaded Context
cwes.desc: The product uses the singleton pattern when creating a resource within a multithreaded environment.

cwes.name: J2EE Misconfiguration: Plaintext Password in Configuration File
cwes.desc: The J2EE application stores a plaintext password in a configuration file.

cwes.name: Unsynchronized Access to Shared Data in a Multithreaded Context
cwes.desc: The product does not properly synchronize shared data, such as static variables across threads, which can lead to undefined behavior and unpredictable data changes.

cwes.name: finalize() Method Without super.finalize()
cwes.desc: The product contains a finalize() method that does not call super.finalize().

cwes.name: Call to Thread run() instead of start()
cwes.desc: The product calls a thread's run() method instead of calling start(), which causes the code to run in the thread of the caller instead of the callee.

cwes.name: EJB Bad Practices: Use of Synchronization Primitives
cwes.desc: The product violates the Enterprise JavaBeans (EJB) specification by using thread synchronization primitives.

cwes.name: EJB Bad Practices: Use of AWT Swing
cwes.desc: The product violates the Enterprise JavaBeans (EJB) specification by using AWT/Swing.

cwes.name: EJB Bad Practices: Use of Java I/O
cwes.desc: The product violates the Enterprise JavaBeans (EJB) specification by using the java.io package.

cwes.name: EJB Bad Practices: Use of Sockets
cwes.desc: The product violates the Enterprise JavaBeans (EJB) specification by using sockets.

cwes.name: EJB Bad Practices: Use of Class Loader
cwes.desc: The product violates the Enterprise JavaBeans (EJB) specification by using the class loader.

cwes.name: J2EE Bad Practices: Non-serializable Object Stored in Session
cwes.desc: The product stores a non-serializable object as an HttpSession attribute, which can hurt reliability.

cwes.name: clone() Method Without super.clone()
cwes.desc: The product contains a clone() method that does not call super.clone() to obtain the new object.

cwes.name: Object Model Violation: Just One of Equals and Hashcode Defined
cwes.desc: The product does not maintain equal hashcodes for equal objects.

cwes.name: Array Declared Public, Final, and Static
cwes.desc: The product declares an array public, final, and static, which is not sufficient to prevent the array's contents from being modified.

cwes.name: finalize() Method Declared Public
cwes.desc: The product violates secure coding principles for mobile code by declaring a finalize() method public.

cwes.name: Empty Synchronized Block
cwes.desc: The product contains an empty synchronized block.

cwes.name: Explicit Call to Finalize()
cwes.desc: The product makes an explicit call to the finalize() method from outside the finalizer.

cwes.name: J2EE Framework: Saving Unserializable Objects to Disk
cwes.desc: When the J2EE container attempts to write unserializable objects to disk there is no guarantee that the process will complete successfully.

cwes.name: Comparison of Object References Instead of Object Contents
cwes.desc: The product compares object references instead of the contents of the objects themselves, preventing it from detecting equivalent objects.

cwes.name: Use of Wrong Operator in String Comparison
cwes.desc: The product uses the wrong operator when comparing a string, such as using == when the .equals() method should be used instead.

cwes.name: Uncaught Exception in Servlet
cwes.desc: The Servlet does not catch all exceptions, which may reveal sensitive debugging information.

cwes.name: Public Static Final Field References Mutable Object
cwes.desc: A public or protected static final field references a mutable object, which allows the object to be changed by malicious code, or accidentally from another package.

cwes.name: Struts: Non-private Field in ActionForm Class
cwes.desc: An ActionForm class contains a field that has not been declared private, which can be accessed without using a setter or getter.

cwes.name: Double-Checked Locking
cwes.desc: The product uses double-checked locking to access a resource without the overhead of explicit synchronization, but the locking is insufficient.

cwes.name: Reachable Assertion
cwes.desc: The product contains an assert() or similar statement that can be triggered by an attacker, which leads to an application exit or other behavior that is more severe than necessary.

cwes.name: Critical Data Element Declared Public
cwes.desc: The product declares a critical variable, field, or member to be public when intended security policy requires it to be private.

cwes.name: Access to Critical Private Variable via Public Method
cwes.desc: The product defines a public method that reads or modifies a private variable.

cwes.name: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an Expression Language Statement ('Expression Language Injection')
cwes.desc: The product constructs all or part of an expression language (EL) statement in a framework such as a Java Server Page (JSP) using externally-influenced input from an upstream component, but it does no

cwes.name: Incorrect Use of Autoboxing and Unboxing for Performance Critical Operations
cwes.desc: The code uses boxed primitives, which may introduce inefficiencies into performance-critical operations.

cwes.name: Incorrect Bitwise Shift of Integer
cwes.desc: An integer value is specified to be shifted by a negative amount or an amount greater than or equal to the number of bits contained in the value causing an unexpected or indeterminate result.

cwes.name: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements Used in a Template Engine
cwes.desc: The product uses a template engine to insert or process externally-influenced input, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements or syntax that can be interpreted as templat

cwes.name: Multiple Releases of Same Resource or Handle
cwes.desc: The product attempts to close or release a resource or handle more than once, without any successful open between the close operations.

cwes.name: Comparison of Incompatible Types
cwes.desc: The product performs a comparison between two entities, but the entities are of different, incompatible types that cannot be guaranteed to provide correct results when they are directly compared.

cwes.name: Improperly Controlled Modification of Object Prototype Attributes ('Prototype Pollution')
cwes.desc: The product receives input from an upstream component that specifies attributes that are to be initialized or updated in an object, but it does not properly control modifications of attributes of the

cwes.name: Improper Neutralization of Directives in Dynamically Evaluated Code ('Eval Injection')

cwes.name: Improper Control of Filename for Include/Require Statement in PHP Program ('PHP Remote File Inclusion')
cwes.desc: The PHP application receives input from an upstream component, but it does not restrict or incorrectly restricts the input before its usage in require, include, or similar functions.

cwes.name: Generation of Error Message Containing Sensitive Information
cwes.desc: The product generates an error message that includes sensitive information about its environment, users, or associated data.

cwes.name: Externally-Generated Error Message Containing Sensitive Information
cwes.desc: The product performs an operation that triggers an external diagnostic or error message that is not directly generated or controlled by the product, such as an error generated by the programming langu

cwes.name: Unrestricted Upload of File with Dangerous Type
cwes.desc: The product allows the upload or transfer of dangerous file types that are automatically processed within its environment.

cwes.name: Insecure Default Variable Initialization
cwes.desc: The product, by default, initializes an internal variable with an insecure or less secure value than is possible.

cwes.name: External Initialization of Trusted Variables or Data Stores
cwes.desc: The product initializes critical internal variables or data stores using inputs that can be modified by untrusted actors.

cwes.name: Use of Uninitialized Variable
cwes.desc: The code uses a variable that has not been initialized, leading to unpredictable or unintended results.

cwes.name: Use of Externally-Controlled Input to Select Classes or Code ('Unsafe Reflection')
cwes.desc: The product uses external input with reflection to select which classes or code to use, but it does not sufficiently prevent the input from selecting improper classes or code.

cwes.name: PHP External Variable Modification
cwes.desc: A PHP application does not properly protect against the modification of variables from external sources, such as query parameters or cookies. This can expose the application to numerous weaknesses tha

cwes.name: Use of Function with Inconsistent Implementations
cwes.desc: The code uses a function that has inconsistent implementations across operating systems and versions.

cwes.name: Omitted Break Statement in Switch
cwes.desc: The product omits a break statement within a switch or similar construct, causing code associated with multiple conditions to execute. This can cause problems when the programmer only intended to exec

cwes.name: Deserialization of Untrusted Data
cwes.desc: The product deserializes untrusted data without sufficiently ensuring that the resulting data will be valid.

cwes.name: Comparison of Object References Instead of Object Contents
cwes.desc: The product compares object references instead of the contents of the objects themselves, preventing it from detecting equivalent objects.

cwes.name: Use of Wrong Operator in String Comparison
cwes.desc: The product uses the wrong operator when comparing a string, such as using == when the .equals() method should be used instead.

cwes.name: Incomplete Identification of Uploaded File Variables (PHP)
cwes.desc: The PHP application uses an old method for processing uploaded files by referencing the four global variables that are set for each file (e.g. $varname, $varname_size, $varname_name, $varname_type). T

cwes.name: Variable Extraction Error
cwes.desc: The product uses external input to determine the names of variables into which information is extracted, without verifying that the names of the specified variables are valid. This could cause the pro

cwes.name: Executable Regular Expression Error
cwes.desc: The product uses a regular expression that either (1) contains an executable component with user-controlled inputs, or (2) allows a user to enable execution by inserting pattern modifiers.

cwes.name: Permissive Regular Expression
cwes.desc: The product uses a regular expression that does not sufficiently restrict the set of allowed values.

cwes.name: Null Byte Interaction Error (Poison Null Byte)
cwes.desc: The product does not properly handle null bytes or NUL characters when passing data between different representations or components.

cwes.name: Dynamic Variable Evaluation
cwes.desc: In a language where the user can influence the name of a variable at runtime, if the variable names are not controlled, an attacker can read or write to arbitrary variables, or access arbitrary functi

cwes.name: Improperly Controlled Modification of Dynamically-Determined Object Attributes
cwes.desc: The product receives input from an upstream component that specifies multiple attributes, properties, or fields that are to be initialized or updated in an object, but it does not properly control whi

cwes.name: Comparison of Incompatible Types
cwes.desc: The product performs a comparison between two entities, but the entities are of different, incompatible types that cannot be guaranteed to provide correct results when they are directly compared.

cwes.name: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements Used in a Template Engine
cwes.desc: The product uses a template engine to insert or process externally-influenced input, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements or syntax that can be interpreted as templat

cwes.name: ASP.NET Misconfiguration: Creating Debug Binary
cwes.desc: Debugging messages help attackers learn about the system and plan a form of attack.

cwes.name: ASP.NET Misconfiguration: Missing Custom Error Page
cwes.desc: An ASP .NET application must enable custom error pages in order to prevent attackers from mining information from the framework's built-in responses.

cwes.name: ASP.NET Misconfiguration: Password in Configuration File
cwes.desc: Storing a plaintext password in a configuration file allows anyone who can read the file access to the password-protected resource making them an easy target for attackers.

cwes.name: Integer Underflow (Wrap or Wraparound)
cwes.desc: The product subtracts one value from another, such that the result is less than the minimum allowable integer value, which produces a value that is not equal to the correct result.

cwes.name: Integer Coercion Error
cwes.desc: Integer coercion refers to a set of flaws pertaining to the type casting, extension, or truncation of primitive data types.

cwes.name: Numeric Truncation Error
cwes.desc: Truncation errors occur when a primitive is cast to a primitive of a smaller size and data is lost in the conversion.

cwes.name: Uncaught Exception
cwes.desc: An exception is thrown from a function, but it is not caught.

cwes.name: Race Condition within a Thread
cwes.desc: If two threads of execution use a resource simultaneously, there exists the possibility that resources may be used while invalid, in turn making the state of execution undefined.

cwes.name: Passing Mutable Objects to an Untrusted Method
cwes.desc: The product sends non-cloned mutable data as an argument to a method or function.

cwes.name: Returning a Mutable Object to an Untrusted Caller
cwes.desc: Sending non-cloned mutable data as a return value may result in that data being altered or deleted by the calling function.

cwes.name: Declaration of Catch for Generic Exception
cwes.desc: Catching overly broad exceptions promotes complex error handling code that is more likely to contain security vulnerabilities.

cwes.name: Declaration of Throws for Generic Exception
cwes.desc: The product throws or raises an overly broad exceptions that can hide important details and produce inappropriate responses to certain conditions.

cwes.name: Unrestricted Upload of File with Dangerous Type
cwes.desc: The product allows the upload or transfer of dangerous file types that are automatically processed within its environment.

cwes.name: Improper Cleanup on Thrown Exception
cwes.desc: The product does not clean up its state or incorrectly cleans up its state when an exception is thrown, leading to unexpected state or control flow.

cwes.name: Duplicate Key in Associative List (Alist)
cwes.desc: Duplicate keys in associative lists can lead to non-unique keys being mistaken for an error.

cwes.name: NULL Pointer Dereference
cwes.desc: The product dereferences a pointer that it expects to be valid but is NULL.
cwes.description: NULL pointer dereference causes application crash.

cwes.name: Missing Default Case in Multiple Condition Expression
cwes.desc: The code does not have a default case in an expression with multiple conditions, such as a switch statement.

cwes.name: Assigning instead of Comparing
cwes.desc: The code uses an operator for assignment when the intention was to perform a comparison.

cwes.name: Omitted Break Statement in Switch
cwes.desc: The product omits a break statement within a switch or similar construct, causing code associated with multiple conditions to execute. This can cause problems when the programmer only intended to exec

cwes.name: Private Data Structure Returned From A Public Method
cwes.desc: The product has a method that is declared public, but returns a reference to a private data structure, which could then be modified in unexpected ways.

cwes.name: Public Data Assigned to Private Array-Typed Field
cwes.desc: Assigning public data to a private array is equivalent to giving public access to the array.

cwes.name: Cloneable Class Containing Sensitive Information
cwes.desc: The code contains a class with sensitive data, but the class is cloneable. The data can then be accessed by cloning the class.

cwes.name: .NET Misconfiguration: Use of Impersonation
cwes.desc: Allowing a .NET application to run at potentially escalated levels of access to the underlying operating and file systems can be dangerous and result in various forms of attacks.

cwes.name: ASP.NET Misconfiguration: Not Using Input Validation Framework
cwes.desc: The ASP.NET application does not use an input validation framework.

cwes.name: ASP.NET Misconfiguration: Use of Identity Impersonation
cwes.desc: Configuring an ASP.NET application to run with impersonated credentials may give the application unnecessary privileges.

cwes.name: Assignment of a Fixed Address to a Pointer
cwes.desc: The product sets a pointer to a specific address other than NULL or 0.

cwes.name: Null Byte Interaction Error (Poison Null Byte)
cwes.desc: The product does not properly handle null bytes or NUL characters when passing data between different representations or components.

cwes.name: Critical Data Element Declared Public
cwes.desc: The product declares a critical variable, field, or member to be public when intended security policy requires it to be private.

cwes.name: Access to Critical Private Variable via Public Method
cwes.desc: The product defines a public method that reads or modifies a private variable.

cwes.name: Improperly Controlled Modification of Dynamically-Determined Object Attributes
cwes.desc: The product receives input from an upstream component that specifies multiple attributes, properties, or fields that are to be initialized or updated in an object, but it does not properly control whi

cwes.name: ASP.NET Misconfiguration: Improper Model Validation
cwes.desc: The ASP.NET application does not use, or incorrectly uses, the model validation framework.

cwes.name: Incorrect Use of Autoboxing and Unboxing for Performance Critical Operations
cwes.desc: The code uses boxed primitives, which may introduce inefficiencies into performance-critical operations.

cwes.name: Incorrect Bitwise Shift of Integer
cwes.desc: An integer value is specified to be shifted by a negative amount or an amount greater than or equal to the number of bits contained in the value causing an unexpected or indeterminate result.

cwes.desc: The product calls a function, procedure, or routine, but the caller specifies too many arguments, or too few arguments, which may lead to undefined behavior and resultant weaknesses.

cwes.name: Function Call With Incorrect Variable or Reference as Argument
cwes.desc: The product calls a function, procedure, or routine, but the caller specifies the wrong variable or reference as one of the arguments, which may lead to undefined behavior and resultant weaknesses.

cwes.desc: The product, while copying or cloning a resource, does not set the resource's permissions or access control until the copy is complete, leaving the resource exposed to other spheres while the copy is

cwes.desc: The product constructs all or part of an SQL command using externally-influenced input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements that could mod

cwes.name: SQL Injection: Hibernate
cwes.desc: Using Hibernate to execute a dynamic SQL statement built with user-controlled input can allow an attacker to modify the statement's meaning or to execute arbitrary SQL commands.

cwes.name: Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled SQL Primary Key
cwes.desc: The product uses a database table that includes records that should not be accessible to an actor, but it executes a SQL statement with a primary key that can be controlled by that actor.

cwes.name: Dangling Database Cursor ('Cursor Injection')
cwes.desc: If a database cursor is not closed properly, then it could become accessible to other users while retaining the same privileges that were originally assigned, leaving the cursor dangling.

cwes.name: Non-SQL Invokable Control Element with Excessive Number of Data Resource Accesses
cwes.desc: The product contains a client with a function or method that contains a large number of data accesses/queries that are sent through a data manager, i.e., does not use efficient database capabilities.

cwes.name: Reachable Assertion
cwes.desc: The product contains an assert() or similar statement that can be triggered by an attacker, which leads to an application exit or other behavior that is more severe than necessary.

cwes.name: Multiple Releases of Same Resource or Handle
cwes.desc: The product attempts to close or release a resource or handle more than once, without any successful open between the close operations.

cwes.name: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm
cwes.desc: The product uses a broken or risky cryptographic algorithm or protocol.

cwes.name: Incorrect Register Defaults or Module Parameters
cwes.desc: Hardware description language code incorrectly defines register defaults or hardware Intellectual Property (IP) parameters to insecure values.

cwes.name: Race Condition for Write-Once Attributes
cwes.desc: A write-once register in hardware design is programmable by an untrusted software component earlier than the trusted software component, resulting in a race condition issue.

cwes.name: Improper Restriction of Write-Once Bit Fields
cwes.desc: The hardware design control register sticky bits or write-once bit fields are improperly implemented, such that they can be reprogrammed by software.

cwes.name: Mirrored Regions with Different Values
cwes.desc: The product's architecture mirrors regions without ensuring that their contents always stay in sync.

cwes.name: Product Released in Non-Release Configuration
cwes.desc: The product released to market is released in pre-production or manufacturing configuration.

cwes.name: Sensitive Information Uncleared Before Debug/Power State Transition
cwes.desc: The product performs a power or debug state transition, but it does not clear sensitive information that should no longer be accessible due to changes to information access restrictions.

cwes.name: Device Unlock Credential Sharing
cwes.desc: The credentials necessary for unlocking a device are shared across multiple parties and may expose sensitive information.

cwes.name: Cryptographic Operations are run Before Supporting Units are Ready
cwes.desc: Performing cryptographic operations without ensuring that the supporting inputs are ready to supply valid data may compromise the cryptographic result.

cwes.name: Access Control Check Implemented After Asset is Accessed
cwes.desc: A product's hardware-based access control check occurs after the asset has been accessed.

cwes.name: Incorrect Chaining or Granularity of Debug Components
cwes.desc: The product's debug components contain incorrect chaining or granularity of debug components.

cwes.name: Unprotected Confidential Information on Device is Accessible by OSAT Vendors
cwes.desc: The product does not adequately protect confidential information on the device from being accessed by Outsourced Semiconductor Assembly and Test (OSAT) vendors.

cwes.name: Hardware Logic Contains Race Conditions
cwes.desc: A race condition in the hardware logic results in undermining security guarantees of the system.

cwes.name: Improper Translation of Security Attributes by Fabric Bridge
cwes.desc: The bridge incorrectly translates security attributes from either trusted to untrusted or from untrusted to trusted when converting from one fabric protocol to another.

cwes.name: Missing Security-Relevant Feedback for Unexecuted Operations in Hardware Interface
cwes.desc: The product has a hardware interface that silently discards operations in situations for which feedback would be security-relevant, such as the timely detection of failures or attacks.

cwes.name: Improper Control of Generation of Code ('Code Injection')
cwes.desc: Lua scripting engine allows runtime code generation (loadstring, dofile); external input can execute arbitrary Lua code. Attack path: Jeep Cherokee Uconnect Lua engine.

cwes.name: Improper Input Validation
cwes.desc: Unvalidated external input (CAN messages, D-Bus arguments) passed to Lua scripts allows logic bypass or arbitrary function calls.
cwes.description: Improper validation of input data allows attacker to inject malformed data.

cwes.name: Uncontrolled Recursion
cwes.desc: Missing recursive call depth limit in Lua causes stack overflow → DoS.

cwes.name: Uncontrolled Resource Consumption
cwes.desc: Lua garbage collector manipulation or infinite loops exhaust ECU resources.
cwes.description: Uncontrolled resource consumption enables denial of service.

cwes.name: Improper Control of Dynamically-Managed Code Resources
cwes.desc: Dynamic module loading (require) in Lua loads malicious modules from untrusted paths.

Lua._note: Lua scripting engine used in the Jeep Cherokee Uconnect head unit (QNX 6.5). Miller & Valasek (2015) achieved arbitrary code execution via D-Bus → Lua injection. PID-LUA1 threat: remote code execution in Lua engine exposed to external input.

PID-34: Device stores logs of applicatio.name: Device stores logs of application events and information

cwes.name: Improper Access Control
cwes.desc: The product does not restrict or incorrectly restricts access to a resource from an unauthorized actor.

cwes.name: Improper Input Validation
cwes.desc: Insufficient input validation in cellular protocol parsers (LTE RRC/NAS, SMS PDU, HTTP over LTE) leads to baseband or application stack code execution.
cwes.description: Improper validation of input data allows attacker to inject malformed data.

cves.description: Qualcomm Snapdragon modem heap overflow in SIP parsing (1xEVDO). Remote code execution via crafted SIP message over cellular. Relevant: Jeep 2015 Uconnect (Qualcomm MDM6200 via Sprint 3G); BMW 2019 TCB (Qualcomm MDM6200 cellular modem).

cwes.name: Missing Encryption of Sensitive Data
cwes.desc: Application-layer encryption absent in vehicle-to-backend cellular communication; GSM A5/0 (no encryption) or A5/1 (weak) may be used.
cwes.description: Sensitive data transmitted or stored without encryption.

cwes.name: Authentication Bypass by Capture-replay
cwes.desc: GSM one-way authentication weakness; IMSI catcher (rogue base station) downgrades vehicle TCU to force an unencrypted connection.
cwes.description: Authentication protocol vulnerable to replay attack.

cves.description: LTE baseband firmware replay attack vector in Qualcomm MSM8996. Replay of legitimate authentication messages allows rogue base-station acceptance.

cwes.name: Missing Authentication for Critical Function
cwes.desc: Insufficient sender authentication for SMS-based vehicle control commands; SMS spoofing delivers unauthorized remote vehicle commands.
cwes.description: Critical functionality accessible without requiring authentication.

cwes.name: Improper Certificate Validation
cwes.desc: Missing server certificate validation on the LTE/HTTPS cellular channel enables MITM attacks.
cwes.description: TLS certificate not properly validated; MitM attack possible.

Cellular (3G/4G/LTE)._note: Cellular (3G/4G/LTE): the remote entry channel for all 5 test cases. Jeep (Sprint 3G), Tesla (LTE), BMW TCB (3G/4G), Mercedes HERMES (3G/4G + eSIM). Remote attack surface (PID-CEL1): rogue base station (TID-CEL102) and direct internet connection (TID-CEL101).

cwes.name: Inadequate Encryption Strength
cwes.desc: Use of weak Wi-Fi encryption (WEP or WPA/TKIP); vehicle Wi-Fi hotspot traffic can be decrypted.
cwes.description: Cryptographic algorithm uses insufficient key length.

cves.description: KRACK (Key Reinstallation Attack) on WPA2 four-way handshake. Allows attacker in Wi-Fi range to decrypt, replay, forge frames. Affects Tesla IVI Wi-Fi (WPA2), BMW HU-Intel Wi-Fi hotspot.

cwes.name: Improper Authentication
cwes.desc: Weak authentication on vehicle Wi-Fi access point (open network or default password); unauthorized access possible at close physical range.
cwes.description: Authentication mechanisms are weak, missing, or bypassable.

cves.description: Wi-Fi FragAttacks: accepts plaintext data frames in protected Wi-Fi network. Bypasses WPA2/WPA3 encryption for fragment injection. Affects automotive Wi-Fi clients with older firmware (Marvell Avastar chipset in Tesla).

cwes.name: Key Exchange without Entity Authentication
cwes.desc: Vehicle Wi-Fi client does not validate AP certificate, enabling Evil Twin AP attack.

cwes.name: Missing Authentication for Critical Function
cwes.desc: IVI services exposed via Wi-Fi lack authentication.
cwes.description: Critical functionality accessible without requiring authentication.

Wi-Fi (802.11)._note: Wi-Fi 802.11: Jeep (b/g), Tesla (802.11), BMW, Mercedes (2.4/5 GHz). Physical-proximity attack channel.

C0051.name: APT28 Nearest Neighbor Campaign
C0051.description_ko: APT28이 2022년 2월 초부터 2024년 11월까지 우크라이나에 대한 전문 지식을 가진 조직 및 개인을 대상으로 수행한 캠페인이다. APT28은 주로 living-off-the-land 기법을 활용하면서 CVE-2022-38028 제로데이 취약점을 악용했다. 특히 목표 대상과 가까운 Wi-Fi 네트워크를 이용하여 피해자 환경에 초기 접근 권한을 획득했다. 목표 대상 근처의 여러 침해 조직을 데이지 체인 방식으로 연결하고, Wi-Fi를 활성화할 수 있는 이중 연결 시스템을 식별한 뒤 탈취한 자격 증명을 사용하여 피해자 네트워크에 연결했다.

cwes.name: Improper Authentication
cwes.description: BLE pairing without sufficient authentication allows rogue device pairing.

cves.description: BIAS (Bluetooth Impersonation AttackS): BR/EDR Secure Connections authentication bypass. Attacker spoofs previously paired device without knowing link key. Relevant to BMW 2019 digital key BLE, Tesla BLE key relay.

cwes.name: Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information
cwes.description: Bluetooth Classic profiles may transmit data without encryption.

cwes.name: Use of Insufficiently Random Values
cwes.description: Weak pairing key generation enables Bluetooth BIAS/KNOB attacks.

cves.description: KNOB attack: Bluetooth BR/EDR entropy negotiation allows attacker to force 1-byte session key. Brute-force in <1 second; affects all Bluetooth devices before spec patch (2020). Vehicle hands-free (HFP) and A2DP profiles affected.

cwes.name: Authentication Bypass by Spoofing
cwes.description: MAC address spoofing to impersonate a trusted Bluetooth device.

cwes.name: Heap-based Buffer Overflow
cwes.description: Heap buffer overflow in Bluetooth profile parser (e.g., libjpeg in PBAP PHOTO handler) enables code execution.

cves.description: VW MIB3 Preh: libjpeg 9c heap overflow in vCard PHOTO scan-line buffer (0x4000 bytes) during PBAP phone book sync via Bluetooth. Allows RCE as phone service. (CVE registered 2023, BH EU 2024)

cwes.name: Stack-based Buffer Overflow
cwes.description: Stack buffer overflow in Bluetooth HFP AT command parser enables code execution.

cves.description: Nissan Leaf BluedragonEvo HFP stack: +ANDROID:probe AT command memcpy to fixed params[10] stack buffer without size check → stack overflow → RCE as root. (Nissan Leaf ZE1, BH Asia 2025)
Nissan Leaf BluedragonEvo HFP: +ANDROID:audiosource AT command stack buffer overflow → RCE as root.
Nissan Leaf BluedragonEvo HFP: +ANDROID:vds AT command stack buffer overflow → RCE as root.

Bluetooth / BLE._note: Bluetooth Classic (BR/EDR) and BLE (4.x/5.x) wireless interface. Automotive uses: hands-free (HFP), audio streaming (A2DP), OBD-II dongles, digital key (PEPS/RKE). Key vulnerabilities: BIAS (authentication bypass), KNOB (key downgrade), BLE relay attacks (vehicle theft). Test cases: BMW 2019 digital key BLE relay, Tesla BLE key relay. VW MIB3 (2024): PBAP vCard PHOTO libjpeg heap overflow (CVE-2023-28905) → RCE as phone service via paired Bluetooth. Nissan Leaf (2025): HFP +ANDROID: AT command stack buffer overflow (CVE-2025-32059/32061/32062) in BluedragonEvo stack running as root → RCE; 0.5-click (Just Works pairing or 2.4GHz jamming).

cwes.name: Improper Certificate Validation
cwes.desc: Missing HTTPS server certificate validation enables MITM; attacker impersonates the OTA update server to distribute malicious firmware.
cwes.description: TLS certificate not properly validated; MitM attack possible.

cves.description: Heartbleed: OpenSSL 1.0.1a–1.0.1f TLS heartbeat extension buffer over-read. Leaks up to 64KB server/client memory including private keys. BMW 2019: ConnectedDrive backend and TCU TLS stack used OpenSSL; Mercedes 2020: backend API servers used OpenSSL; Tesla OTA HTTPS channel.
Erlang/OTP TLS server accepts wildcard certificate without proper validation. MITM on TLS connections. Affects embedded OTP/MQTT brokers in telematics stacks.

cwes.name: Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information
cwes.desc: Vehicle control commands or location data transmitted over plain HTTP (no encryption) → susceptible to sniffing.
cwes.description: Sensitive data sent in cleartext over unencrypted channel.

cves.description: POODLE: TLS 1.0 downgrade to SSLv3 padding oracle. Decrypts session cookies via CBC padding oracle with ~256 requests. Automotive backend systems with SSLv3 fallback (pre-2015) affected.

cwes.name: URL Redirection to Untrusted Site ('Open Redirect')
cwes.desc: Unvalidated URL redirect in the in-vehicle browser or OTA client; attacker redirects to a malicious server delivering malicious content.
cwes.description: Unvalidated redirect/forward; user directed to malicious site.

cwes.name: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)
cwes.desc: CSRF vulnerability in an in-vehicle HTTP service (management web interface); malicious web page changes vehicle configuration.

PID-4111: Lacks protocol support for mes.name: Device lacks protocol support for message authentication

cwes.name: Missing Authentication for Critical Function (Base)
cwes.desc: The product does not perform any authentication for functionality that requires a provable user identity or consumes a significant amount of resources.

cwes.name: Improper Authentication (Class)
cwes.desc: When an actor claims to have a given identity, the product does not prove or insufficiently proves that the claim is correct.

PID-4112: Lacks protocol support for mes.name: Device lacks protocol support for message encryption

cwes.name: Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information
cwes.desc: The product transmits sensitive or security-critical data in cleartext in a communication channel that can be sniffed by unauthorized actors.

PID-4113: Includes cryptographic functio.name: Device includes cryptographic functions for sensitive data, such as encryption or authentication

cwes.name: Authentication Bypass by Capture-replay
cwes.desc: A capture-replay flaw exists when the design of the product makes it possible for a malicious user to sniff network traffic and bypass authentication by replaying it to the server in question to the same effect as the original message (or with minor changes).

cwes.name: Improper Certificate Validation
cwes.desc: The product does not validate, or incorrectly validates, a certificate.

cwes.name: Insufficient Entropy (Base)
cwes.desc: The product uses an algorithm or scheme that produces insufficient entropy, leaving patterns or clusters of values that are more likely to occur than others.

cwes.name: Use of Cryptographically Weak Pseudo-Random Number Generator (PRNG) (Base)
cwes.desc: The product uses a Pseudo-Random Number Generator (PRNG) in a security context, but the PRNG's algorithm is not cryptographically strong.

cwes.name: Use of a Cryptographic Primitive with a Risky Implementation
cwes.desc: To fulfill the need for a cryptographic primitive, the product implements a cryptographic algorithm using a non-standard, unproven, or disallowed/non-compliant cryptographic implementation.

cwes.name: Exposure of Sensitive Information Through Metadata
cwes.desc: The product prevents direct access to a resource containing sensitive information, but it does not sufficiently limit access to metadata that is derived from the original, sensitive information.

cwes.name: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm
cwes.desc: The product uses a broken or risky cryptographic algorithm or protocol.

cwes.name: External Control of System or Configuration Setting
cwes.desc: One or more system settings or configuration elements can be externally controlled by a user.

cwes.name: Improper Authorization
cwes.desc: The product does not perform or incorrectly performs an authorization check when an actor attempts to access a resource or perform an action.

cwes.name: ICS Supply Chain: Poorly Documented or Undocumented Features
cwes.desc: The product contains features that are not sufficiently documented, which can create entry points for unauthorized actors.

cwes.name: Hidden Functionality (Class)
cwes.desc: The product contains functionality that is not documented, not part of the specification, and not accessible through an interface or command sequence that is obvious to the product's users or administrators.

cwes.name: Insufficient Technical Documentation
cwes.desc: The product does not contain sufficient technical or engineering documentation (whether on paper or in electronic form) that contains descriptions of all the relevant software/hardware elements of the product, such as its usage, structure, architectural components, interfaces, design, implementation, configuration, operation, etc.

cwes.name: Deadlock
cwes.desc: The product contains multiple threads or executable segments that are waiting for each other to release a necessary lock, resulting in deadlock.

cwes.name: Uncontrolled Resource Consumption
cwes.desc: The product does not properly control the allocation and maintenance of a resource that can be consumed by an actor.

cwes.name: Insufficient Resource Pool
cwes.desc: The product's resource pool is not large enough to handle peak demand, which allows an attacker to prevent others from accessing the resource by using a (relatively) large number of requests for resources.

cwes.name: Authentication Bypass by Capture-replay (Base)
cwes.desc: A capture-replay flaw exists when the design of the product makes it possible for a malicious user to sniff network traffic and bypass authentication by replaying it to the server in question to the same effect as the original message (or with minor changes).

PID-411: Remote services with sensitive.name: Device exposes remote services with the ability to send, receive, view or modify sensitive information or configurations

HTTP / HTTPS._note: HTTP/HTTPS used for vehicle-to-backend OTA updates, remote diagnostics, and telematics API calls. Missing certificate validation (CWE-295) or cleartext transmission (CWE-319) enables man-in-the-middle attacks. Applicable to Tesla OTA, BMW NGTP HTTP wrapper, and Mercedes backend API.

C0051.name: APT28 Nearest Neighbor Campaign
C0051.description_ko: APT28이 2022년 2월 초부터 2024년 11월까지 우크라이나에 대한 전문 지식을 가진 조직 및 개인을 대상으로 수행한 캠페인이다. APT28은 주로 living-off-the-land 기법을 활용하면서 CVE-2022-38028 제로데이 취약점을 악용했다. 특히 목표 대상과 가까운 Wi-Fi 네트워크를 이용하여 피해자 환경에 초기 접근 권한을 획득했다. 목표 대상 근처의 여러 침해 조직을 데이지 체인 방식으로 연결하고, Wi-Fi를 활성화할 수 있는 이중 연결 시스템을 식별한 뒤 탈취한 자격 증명을 사용하여 피해자 네트워크에 연결했다.

cwes.name: Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information
cwes.description: MQTT without TLS transmits telemetry data in plaintext.

cwes.name: Improper Authentication
cwes.description: MQTT broker with no/weak authentication allows unauthorized publish/subscribe.

cwes.name: Uncontrolled Resource Consumption
cwes.description: Unauthenticated MQTT publish floods broker, causing denial of service.

cwes.name: Missing Authentication for Critical Function
cwes.description: CoAP endpoints without DTLS allow unauthenticated resource manipulation.

MQTT / CoAP._note: MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport, ISO/IEC 20922) and CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol, RFC 7252). Lightweight IoT/V2X telematics messaging protocols. Without TLS/DTLS: cleartext transmission; without mTLS: unauthorized publish/subscribe. Growing attack surface in future connected-vehicle V2X infrastructure.

Erlang/OTP TLS server accepts wildcard certificate without proper validation. MITM on TLS connections. Affects embedded OTP/MQTT brokers in telematics stacks.

cwes.desc: A capture-replay flaw exists when the design of the product makes it possible for a malicious user to sniff network traffic and bypass authentication by replaying it to the server in question to the same effect as the original message (or with minor changes).

cwes.name: Improper Certificate Validation
cwes.desc: Missing HTTPS server certificate validation enables MITM; attacker impersonates the OTA update server to distribute malicious firmware.
cwes.description: TLS certificate not properly validated; MitM attack possible.

cves.description: Heartbleed: OpenSSL 1.0.1a–1.0.1f TLS heartbeat extension buffer over-read. Leaks up to 64KB server/client memory including private keys. BMW 2019: ConnectedDrive backend and TCU TLS stack used OpenSSL; Mercedes 2020: backend API servers used OpenSSL; Tesla OTA HTTPS channel.
Erlang/OTP TLS server accepts wildcard certificate without proper validation. MITM on TLS connections. Affects embedded OTP/MQTT brokers in telematics stacks.

cwes.name: Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information
cwes.desc: Vehicle control commands or location data transmitted over plain HTTP (no encryption) → susceptible to sniffing.
cwes.description: Sensitive data sent in cleartext over unencrypted channel.

cves.description: POODLE: TLS 1.0 downgrade to SSLv3 padding oracle. Decrypts session cookies via CBC padding oracle with ~256 requests. Automotive backend systems with SSLv3 fallback (pre-2015) affected.

cwes.name: URL Redirection to Untrusted Site ('Open Redirect')
cwes.desc: Unvalidated URL redirect in the in-vehicle browser or OTA client; attacker redirects to a malicious server delivering malicious content.
cwes.description: Unvalidated redirect/forward; user directed to malicious site.

cwes.name: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)
cwes.desc: CSRF vulnerability in an in-vehicle HTTP service (management web interface); malicious web page changes vehicle configuration.
